title: The Each Iterator 🧱
description: Meet Ruby's favorite and most powerful way to loop through data.

slides.title: The Ruby Way 💎
slides.content:
# The `.each` Method

In most languages, the `for` loop is king.
In Ruby, the `.each` method is king.

Rather than building a clunky loop structure, `.each` is a method that you attach directly onto a piece of data (like a range).

slides.title: Syntax Style
slides.content:
Notice how this looks just like `.times`. You are passing a block of code (using `do` and `end`) to the `.each` method.

```ruby
(1..5).each do |num|
  puts "Value is: #{num}"
end
```

slides.title: Each in Action
slides.content: Run this code. Just like the For loop, the variable between the pipes `| |` catches each number iteratively.

quizData.question: What is `.each`?

options.text: A built-in method that grabs items out of a collection one-by-one.
A standalone command that generates a random number.

quizData.explanation: It is a built in method. It "iterates" (steps through) any sequence of data and passes that data to its attached block.

slides.title: Iterators vs Loops 🥊
slides.content:
`while`, `until`, and `for` are traditional loops.
`.each` and `.times` are called **Iterators**.

An iterator is a Ruby method that repeatedly invokes a block of code.

Because they are methods, iterators treat everything as an object, making your code safer, cleaner, and vastly more "Ruby-like".

quizData.question: Which of these is the correct syntax for a block parameter in an iterator?
quizData.explanation: Iterators use the pipe `|` symbols to define variables generated by the method.

slides.title: Curly Braces Block { }
slides.content:
Just like the single-line if statement, there is a single-line block syntax!

You can replace `do...end` with curly braces `{...}`.

`(1..5).each {|n| puts n }`
